clarity.name: Clarity
clarity.description: How clear and understandable is the communication?

levels.1: Very unclear, hard to follow, disorganized thoughts
levels.2: Somewhat unclear, some parts hard to follow
levels.3: Mostly clear, minor lapses in clarity
levels.4: Clear and easy to follow throughout
levels.5: Exceptionally clear, every point is well-articulated

fluency.name: Fluency
fluency.description: Smoothness and flow of communication without excessive pausing or repetition

levels.1: Very choppy, many long pauses or repetitions
levels.3: Mostly fluent with occasional pauses
levels.5: Exceptionally smooth and natural flow

vocabulary.name: Vocabulary
vocabulary.description: Range and appropriateness of words used

levels.1: Very limited vocabulary, many inappropriate word choices
levels.3: Adequate vocabulary, mostly appropriate
levels.4: Good range of vocabulary, well-chosen words
levels.5: Excellent, rich vocabulary used precisely and effectively

confidence.name: Confidence
confidence.description: Level of assurance and authority in delivery

levels.3: Generally confident with some uncertain moments
levels.5: Exceptionally confident and commanding presence

structure.name: Structure
structure.description: Logical organization with clear beginning, middle, and end

levels.2: Weak structure, hard to identify sections
levels.3: Basic structure present but not fully developed
levels.5: Excellent, well-organized with seamless transitions

grammar.name: Grammar
grammar.description: Correctness of grammar, punctuation, and sentence structure

levels.1: Many serious grammar errors that impede understanding
levels.2: Several grammar errors, some impact on clarity
levels.3: Some grammar errors but meaning is clear
levels.5: Grammatically excellent with no notable errors

tone.name: Tone
tone.description: Appropriateness of tone to the context and audience

levels.1: Completely inappropriate tone for the context
levels.3: Generally appropriate tone with minor lapses
levels.5: Perfect tone, precisely calibrated to context and audience

persuasion.name: Persuasion
persuasion.description: Effectiveness at convincing or influencing the audience

levels.1: Not at all persuasive, no clear argument
levels.3: Some persuasive elements, moderately convincing
levels.4: Persuasive and convincing with good support
levels.5: Highly persuasive, compelling and well-supported

reasoning.name: Reasoning
reasoning.description: Quality of logical reasoning and critical thinking

levels.2: Weak reasoning, limited support for claims
levels.3: Some logical reasoning present
levels.5: Excellent critical reasoning with strong evidence

storytelling.name: Storytelling
storytelling.description: Ability to craft engaging narratives with emotional resonance

levels.3: Decent story with some engaging elements
levels.5: Compelling story with vivid details and emotional impact

completeness.name: Completeness
completeness.description: Whether all required elements are present and addressed

levels.3: Most elements present, some gaps
levels.5: Complete and thorough coverage of all elements

descriptiveness.name: Descriptiveness
descriptiveness.description: Use of vivid, sensory details that paint a clear picture

levels.3: Some good details, could be more vivid
levels.5: Rich, vivid descriptions that immerse the reader

argumentation.name: Argumentation
argumentation.description: Strength and logic of arguments presented

levels.3: Some valid arguments, needs more support
levels.5: Excellent, well-structured and compelling arguments

formality.name: Formality
formality.description: Appropriate level of formal language for the context

levels.3: Mix of formal and informal, generally acceptable
levels.5: Perfectly formal, professional throughout

coherence.name: Coherence
coherence.description: Logical flow and connection between ideas

levels.3: Some logical connections, some gaps
levels.4: Ideas flow well with good transitions
levels.5: Perfectly coherent, seamless flow of ideas

creativity.name: Creativity
creativity.description: Originality and creative approach to the task

levels.3: Some creative touches, mostly conventional
levels.5: Highly creative, original and imaginative approach

analysis.name: Analysis
analysis.description: Depth of analytical thinking and examination of the topic

levels.1: Surface level only, no real analysis
levels.3: Some analytical thinking, could go deeper
levels.5: Excellent, deep and nuanced analysis

narrative-flow.name: Narrative Flow
narrative-flow.description: How well the story progresses and engages the reader

levels.3: Decent flow with some rough transitions
levels.5: Excellent flow, story pulls the reader in seamlessly

originality.name: Originality
originality.description: Uniqueness and fresh perspective in approach

levels.2: Mostly predictable with few original ideas
levels.3: Some original elements amid conventional ideas
levels.5: Highly original, unique and memorable

critical-thinking.name: Critical Thinking
critical-thinking.description: Ability to evaluate, synthesize, and form independent judgments

levels.3: Some evidence of critical thinking
levels.5: Excellent critical thinking, sophisticated analysis

recommendations.clarity: Practice the PREP method (Point, Reason, Example, Point) to structure your thoughts before speaking or writing.
recommendations.fluency: Record yourself speaking daily for 5 minutes on any topic. Review and identify hesitation patterns.
recommendations.vocabulary: Read 20 minutes daily from varied sources. Keep a vocabulary journal of new words.
recommendations.confidence: Join a public speaking group (like Toastmasters) or practice mirror exercises daily.
recommendations.structure: Use the 'rule of three' — every communication should have 3 main points with a clear intro and conclusion.
recommendations.grammar: Use grammar checking tools and study common error patterns. Practice editing your own writing.
recommendations.tone: Practice code-switching — write the same message in formal and informal registers and compare them.
recommendations.persuasion: Study rhetorical techniques: ethos, pathos, logos. Practice by writing persuasive pieces on daily topics.
recommendations.reasoning: Practice structured argumentation using the Toulmin model: claim, grounds, warrant, backing.
recommendations.storytelling: Learn the three-act structure and apply it to personal anecdotes. Practice storytelling in conversations.
recommendations.completeness: Use checklists before submitting written communication. Review prompts against your responses.
recommendations.descriptiveness: Practice sensory writing exercises — describe a scene using all five senses.
recommendations.argumentation: Debate both sides of an argument to understand opposing views and strengthen your own position.
recommendations.formality: Study formal writing templates and practice rewriting informal messages in formal register.
recommendations.coherence: Use transition words and phrases (however, therefore, in addition) to link your ideas.
recommendations.creativity: Practice free-writing for 10 minutes daily without editing. Read widely across genres.
recommendations.analysis: Practice the PEEL method (Point, Evidence, Explanation, Link) for analytical writing.
recommendations.narrative-flow: Study narrative techniques like foreshadowing, pacing, and scene transitions in good literature.
recommendations.originality: Challenge yourself to find a non-obvious angle on every topic. Ask 'What would most people NOT say?'
recommendations.critical-thinking: Practice the Socratic questioning method — question assumptions, evidence, and implications.
